title: What is Pooling?
concept: Pooling downsamples a feature map by summarizing small local regions with a fixed rule, unlike convolution, which uses learned weights to compute each output value.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What makes a convolution layer different from a simple fixed rule?

options.text: A convolution uses learned filter weights to compute each output value
A convolution always keeps the biggest number in a region
A convolution never changes the numbers in a feature map

exercises.explain: Exactly. Convolution learns weights. Pooling is the next idea: summarizing with a fixed rule instead.

cards.title: From detailed map to summary map
cards.body: Imagine you have a huge city map covered in tiny notes. If you only need the big picture, you might combine each small neighborhood into one quick summary. That is the job of pooling: take a large feature map and shrink it into a smaller summary map.

cards.title: What is pooling?
cards.body: Pooling is a layer that downsamples a feature map by looking at a small window, such as 2x2, and replacing that whole region with one summary number. Unlike convolution, pooling has no learned weights — it just follows a fixed rule like max or average.

cards.title: Packing bins into one label
cards.body: Picture four storage bins sitting together. Instead of writing down every item in all four bins, you stick on one label that summarizes the group. Pooling does that to nearby activations: many numbers in, one summary number out.

cards.title: One window, one summary
cards.body: Suppose a small patch of a feature map contains [3, 1; 0, 4]. A pooling layer does not multiply these by learned weights. It simply summarizes them. With max pooling, that window becomes 4. With average pooling, it becomes 2.

cards.title: Pooling is not "another convolution"
cards.body: It is tempting to think pooling is just a weaker kind of convolution. It is not. Convolution asks, "How strongly does this learned pattern match here?" Pooling asks, "How can I summarize this nearby region into fewer numbers?" Different job, different mechanism.

cards.title: Convolution vs. Pooling
cards.body: Both slide over local regions, but they do different work:

left.text:
Uses learned weights
detects patterns
computes a new response

right.text:
Uses a fixed rule
summarizes nearby values
shrinks the map

options.text: It summarizes small regions of a feature map into fewer numbers
It learns brand new filter weights for every window
It converts a feature map back into RGB pixels
It always makes the feature map larger

exercises.explain: Right — pooling is about summarizing local neighborhoods and shrinking spatial size.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Pooling uses learned weights in the same way convolution does.
exercises.explain: False. Pooling follows a fixed summary rule, such as max or average, instead of learning weights.

exercises.sentence: Pooling downsamples a feature map by summarizing local ___.
exercises.explain: Regions is right — pooling looks at nearby patches and replaces each patch with one summary value.

exercises.prompt: Which statement shows the difference between convolution and pooling most clearly?

options.text: Convolution uses learned filters to detect patterns, while pooling uses a fixed rule to summarize nearby values
Convolution and pooling are exactly the same except for the layer name
Pooling learns edge detectors, while convolution only averages numbers

exercises.explain: Exactly. Convolution detects with learned weights; pooling summarizes with a fixed rule.

exercises.prompt: A CNN has already used convolution to create a feature map full of edge detections. Now the designer wants a smaller map that still keeps a summary of each nearby region. Which next step matches pooling?

options.text: Slide a small window across the feature map and replace each window with one fixed-rule summary value, such as a max or average
Train a brand new RGB camera to re-photograph the feature map
Multiply each window by newly learned weights so the layer can detect a different pattern
Copy every value unchanged so the map stays the same size

exercises.explain: Exactly. Pooling comes after the feature map already exists, and its job is to summarize nearby values with a fixed rule. Next: the most common fixed rule of all — max pooling.
